options: Marked compression or sudden compression-decompression during delivery, including iatrogenic causes
s2.explain: Birth trauma arises from the 3 P's (Passages, Passenger, Power) plus iatrogenic causes (instrumental delivery, intrauterine manipulation).

s2.q: Which of the following is an iatrogenic cause of birth trauma?
s2.explain: Iatrogenic: instrumental delivery (ventouse, forceps) and intrauterine manipulation (internal podalic version, breech extraction).

s2.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s2.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s2.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s2.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s2.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s2.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s3.q: The most frequent birth fracture is of the:
s3.explain: Hierarchy of frequency: 1. Clavicle, 2. Humerus, 3. Femur, 4. Skull bones. Clavicular fractures are best treated conservatively.

s3.q: Skull fractures in the newborn are considered more serious because:
options: There is a possibility of underlying hemorrhage
s3.explain: Skull fractures: possibility of underlying hemorrhage must be considered. The majority of neonatal fractures heal with conservative treatment.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

options: Depends on the specific condition and classification system
s3.explain: Classification varies by condition — each has its own typing system.

s3.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s3.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

options: Edema of the scalp present at birth
Bleeding between the periosteum and skull bone
s4.explain: Cephalohematoma = bleeding between the periosteum and skull bone, taking the shape of the underlying bone. Develops hours/days after birth.

s4.q: Which is a key distinguishing feature of cephalohematoma compared to caput succedaneum?
options: Develops hours or days after birth
Is always pathological and requires drainage
s4.explain: Cephalohematoma develops hours-days after birth (vs present at birth for caput), does NOT cross suture lines (caput does), and is usually benign.

s4.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s4.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s4.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s4.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.
Most classifications combine anatomical and clinical parameters.

s5.q: Erb-Duchenne palsy is caused by injury to nerve roots:
s5.explain: Erb-Duchenne palsy = C5-C6 (upper trunk). Klumpke's palsy = C8-T1 (lower trunk). Total arm paralysis = C5-T1.

s5.q: Klumpke's palsy affects which nerve roots?
s5.explain: Klumpke's palsy = C8-T1 (lower trunk), affecting hand and forearm muscles. Erb-Duchenne = C5-C6.

s5.q: The prognosis for most brachial plexus injuries at birth is:
s5.explain: Most neonatal brachial plexus injuries recover spontaneously with conservative management (physiotherapy). Surgery is reserved for persistent cases.

s5.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s5.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s5.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s5.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

options: Difficult delivery, especially with hyperextension of the neck or breech extraction
s6.explain: Sternomastoid tumor results from difficult delivery with neck hyperextension or breech extraction, causing hematoma in the sternomastoid muscle.
Conservative: passive stretching exercises and physiotherapy. Most resolve spontaneously. Surgery only if persistent after 1 year.

options: Usually heal well with conservative measures including cleansing and steri-strips
Are always due to ventouse cup detachment
s7.explain: Most facial/soft tissue lacerations heal well with conservative management: cleansing, steri-strips, and observation.

s7.q: Scalp lacerations from fetal scalp electrodes or ventouse application:
options: Usually heal well with local cleansing and monitoring for infection
s7.explain: Scalp lacerations typically heal well with local cleansing and infection monitoring. The main concern is infection (cellulitis, abscess).
